coredata.dc:title: Probing the tunable optical properties of highly luminescent functionalized graphene quantum dots as downconverters for superior detection of ultraviolet radiation
coredata.dc:creator: Jana, Subhajit
Dey, Tamal
Shivakiran Bhaktha B. N.,
Ray, Samit K.
coredata.dc:description: Detection and utilization of ultraviolet (UV) radiation are of paramount importance as commonly used Si photodetectors and photovoltaic cells suffer from poor UV response. Herein, we demonstrate the applications of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) as downconverters to sensitize Si photodiodes for extended and enhanced UV detection down to 300 nm and facilitating distinct visible response to UV wavelength-specific excitation. Stable blue (B-) and orange-red (R-) emitting nitrogen functionalized GQDs, synthesized by cost-effective solvothermal method, exhibit high photoluminescence (PL) quantum yield (57–62%), strong UV absorption, and large Stokes shift (130–250 nm). A scalable threefold improvement in UV response (330 nm) with a relative enhancement factor of 200% is recorded from a Si photodetector externally sensitized with visible-blind B-GQDs. The engineered combination of B- and R-GQDs in a tandem fashion exhibits distinct visible emission and facilitates bare-eye recognition of UV sub-bands. The present study provides a new pathway of UV detection with or without integrated devices by employing thin layers of solution-processed GQDs.
coredata.dcterms:subject: GQDs
Doped GQDs
Downconverters
UV detection
UV band sensor

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdateend pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure e-component body mmlmath acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantsponsor grantsponsorid orcid primabst ref specialabst

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Ministry of Electronics and Information Technology

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a,b) TEM micrographs showing homogeneous, monodispersed B- and R-GQDs. (c) XPS survey spectra revealing the elements and impurity-free nature of the samples. High-resolution core-level XPS spectra (deconvoluted) of C 1s electrons of (d) undoped and (e) N-doped GQDs showing different contributions from various functional groups. The absence of the C–N bond can be noted in the undoped sample. (f) XPS spectrum (deconvoluted) for N 1s electrons of N-doped GQDs showing different configurations of nitrogen bonding. The ratio of pyrrolic-N to graphitic-N is estimated to be ∼2.3, suggesting the dominance of the pyrrolic ring in R-GQDs. No signal from N is detected from the undoped sample, even in a core-level high-resolution scan, as can be visualized in Fig. S3b, SI.

(a) UV–vis–NIR absorption and PL spectra of the doped and undoped GQDs. Inset shows the digital photograph of the colloidal dispersion in quartz cuvette under 325 nm laser excitation. Both the samples have peak absorption in the UV range, and emission is spread over the visible band. A huge shift of PL peak position between B- and R-GQDs is realized by N-doping. (b) Variation of PLQY of the samples with time and doping concentration. The samples show excellent luminescent behavior. (c) PL decay profile of the samples indicating that the radiative decay rate is higher in N-doped GQDs, and (d) Raman shift of the GQDs showing characteristic ‘D’ and ‘G’ bands.

(a) Schematic representation of the downshifting photodetector. In the inset, a digital photograph of the Si photodiode (Si PD) is presented. (b) Optical absorption, PL, and PLE spectra of B-GQDs film deposited by spin-coating technique. The inset shows the bright (visible) emission from B-GQDs film under UV (325 nm) excitation. The area that appears white in the digital image is owing to the saturation of camera sensor due to high light intensity. The PLE spectrum closely resembles the absorption spectrum shown in Fig. 2a. A comparison of the optical absorption spectra of the colloidal GQDs and coated film is shown in Fig. S5, SI. (c) The transparency characteristics of B-GQD film for visible-blind applications. The film is found to maintain a high level of transparency. (d) Spectral variation of the photoresponse of bare Si photodiode compared to the one sensitized with B-GQDs. (e) Relative enhancement of responsivity of the downshifting photodetector showing ∼200% an enhancement of photoresponse at 300 nm. (f) A zoomed-in view of the responsivity of the modified Si PD in the UV region for different thicknesses of the GQDs downconverting layer. F1 (∼100 nm), F2 (∼140 nm), and F3 (∼180 nm) are films of B-GQDs of different thicknesses. An approximately threefold enhancement of responsivity is achieved for F2 sample. (g) Schematic arrangement of the UV light recognition system using an unmodified Si PD mounted parallel to an LP filter and with or without the thin film/dispersion of GQDs. The distance between the GQD film and the PD is kept fixed at 5 mm. The LP filter cuts off the UV light (<350 nm) from reaching the Si photodiode (Fig. S8, SI). (h) LED as an indicator of different excitations (UV or visible) demonstrating the downconverting applications of GQDs.

Demonstration of a UV sensor exhibiting distinct visible emissions specific to UV wavelength. (a) Schematic representation of the working principle of the UV sensor with the films deposited on a glass substrate (B-GQDs/R-GQDs/glass). (b) Excitation wavelength-dependent PL spectra of the tandem downconverter showing the shift in emission peak position. (c) Variation of the emission peak of the UV sensor with different UV excitations. Adjacent digital photographs show the naked-eye visualization of different color emission corresponding to a UV wavelength. In the wavelength region under test, different UV sub-bands (UV-A and -B) specific excitation results in naked-eye UV detection. The area that appears in white in digital images is owing to the saturation of the camera sensor due to the high intensity of light.

head.ce:title: Probing the tunable optical properties of highly luminescent functionalized graphene quantum dots as downconverters for superior detection of ultraviolet radiation

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physics, Indian Institute of Technology Kharagpur, Kharagpur, 721 302, WB, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Physics, Indian Institute of Technology Kharagpur, Kharagpur-721 302, WB, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Nanoscience and Technology, Indian Institute of Technology Kharagpur, Kharagpur, 721 302, WB, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Nanoscience and Technology, Indian Institute of Technology Kharagpur, Kharagpur-721 302, WB, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Detection and utilization of ultraviolet (UV) radiation are of paramount importance as commonly used Si photodetectors and photovoltaic cells suffer from poor UV response. Herein, we demonstrate the applications of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) as downconverters to sensitize Si photodiodes for extended and enhanced UV detection down to 300 nm and facilitating distinct visible response to UV wavelength-specific excitation. Stable blue (B-) and orange-red (R-) emitting nitrogen functionalized GQDs, synthesized by cost-effective solvothermal method, exhibit high photoluminescence (PL) quantum yield (57–62%), strong UV absorption, and large Stokes shift (130–250 nm). A scalable threefold improvement in UV response (330 nm) with a relative enhancement factor of 200% is recorded from a Si photodetector externally sensitized with visible-blind B-GQDs. The engineered combination of B- and R-GQDs in a tandem fashion exhibits distinct visible emission and facilitates bare-eye recognition of UV sub-bands. The present study provides a new pathway of UV detection with or without integrated devices by employing thin layers of solution-processed GQDs.

Studies of functionalized graphene quantum dots (GQDs) with tunable optical properties for photon downconversion is reported here. A downconverting photodetection scheme is demonstrated using the GQDs to enhance the ultraviolet response of commercial Si photodiodes. Further, using the tunable optical properties of GQDs, an ultraviolet band sensor is demonstrated that facilitates distinct visible response in excitation with ultraviolet light of different sub-bands.
Image 1

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Highly efficient and stable photonic devices exhibiting multicolor emission and detection are of great significance for integrated applications, as most active materials only respond to a specific band [1–3]. Numerous efforts have been made to detect broadband light using engineered combinations of different materials with optical absorption spreading across a wider spectral range with improved efficacy [4–8]. In this regard, highly responsive Si broadband photodetectors are extensively used because of their well-established fabrication process, stability, reliability, and durability [9–13]. Particularly, sensing and imaging ultraviolet (UV) light are of special interest because of their diverse negative impact on the health of living organisms and numerous applications [14–16]. Direct and prolonged exposure to UV lights can cause serious damage to the skin and soft tissues like the eyes. However, the UV response (<400 nm) of the widely used Si photodetectors is limited, suffering from high reflection and low penetration depth [17]. Although various wide band gap semiconductors, such as metal oxides (i.e., ZnO, TiO2, etc.) [18,19] and III-nitrides (i.e., GaN, AlN, etc.) [15,20,21], have been widely used to detect UV light, low-cost alternative approaches compatible with existing technologies would be beneficial. In recent years, efforts have been made to convert UV light to visible using downconverting luminescent materials coupled to visible photodetectors for superior UV detection. Such technological advancements using a downconverting layer externally integrated with a visible photosensor have been made possible using solution-processed two-dimensional (2D) nanomaterials [22], perovskites [23,24], and other inorganic semiconductor nanocrystals or quantum dots (QDs) [25,26] exhibiting high visible photoluminescence (PL) quantum yield (QY). While the scheme is promising, good UV detection originates at the cost of degraded performance in the visible region, as absorption loss in the downconverting film is inevitable in some cases [24,25]. In this regard, highly luminescent non-toxic graphene QDs (GQDs) are significant because of their size-tunable optical absorption and emission spectra with large Stokes shift. Taking advantage of the solution-based synthesis processes, such optical properties can even be largely tuned by external doping and functionalization [22]. GQDs exhibit good UV-sensing properties in terms of photodetectors [27,28] and enhanced performance of photovoltaic cells [29,30]. Integration of GQDs as UV-absorber with Si-photodetectors in a heterojunction platform or surface sensitization are proven effective toward better-quality UV sensors [27,31]. Indeed by considering the effectiveness of GQDs for downconverting applications, a detailed study to utilize their potential for such applications is desirable.

Besides the harmful effects, humans do need moderate exposure to UV light for the natural synthesis of vitamin-D and immunity boosting [32], and there is a high demand in grounds like communication [23], water purification, disinfection [33], etc. Therefore, UV sensing with respect to different sub-bands (i.e., UV-A, -B, and -C) is of great importance as the lights of these short-wavelength regions exhibit different penetration depths and diverse effects with respect to energy and intensity. For instance, the UV window to earth's atmosphere, a certain portion of the solar UV light in the wavelength range of 300–400 nm, can reach to the ground owing to the high penetration depth [34]. Recently, Saha et al. [35] demonstrated a novel UV-band discriminator exhibiting distinct visible responses specific to UV sub-bands using stacked layers of Mg-doped ZnO QDs without requiring complex optics. Tunable absorption and emission characteristics have been achieved through band-gap engineering by varying doping concentrations, and the established protocols would enable detection of UV radiation beyond regular optoelectronics. Herein, we report the sensitization of commercial Si photodiodes with highly luminescent GQDs for extended and enhanced UV detection down to 300 nm and demonstrate the naked eye visualization of distinct visible response specific to UV sub-bands. UV absorbing and blue (B-) and orange-red (R-) emitting GQDs with high PLQY (62%) have been synthesized by means of cost-effective solvothermal routes. With the introduction of nitrogen functionalization, PLQY and Stokes shift of the R-GQDs are found to increase significantly compared to the N-free counterpart. A commercial Si photodiode is found to exhibit a three-fold improved UV response (at 320 nm) with a relative enhancement factor of 200% upon sensitization with visible blind B-GQDs. A thin film consisting of tandem style dual-layered structure of the GQDs (B/R) exhibits UV wavelength specific tunable excitation-emission characteristics and facilitates naked eye recognition of UV-light on a sub-band basis. The protocols demonstrated here would be beneficial for UV detection utilizing solution-processed nanomaterials with or without complex optics and integrated devices.

A simple one-pot solvothermal route was used to synthesize the pristine GQDs (B-GQDs) and N-doped GQDs (R-GQDs). Initially, 210 mg citric acid monohydrate (CA; C6H8O7.H2O) was mixed with 160 mg sodium hydroxide (NaOH) in 5 mL of deionized water and stirred well until the reagents were fully dissolved. The as-obtained mixture was put into a Teflon-made autoclave chamber and was heated in a closed furnace at 180 °C for 6 h. After that, 2 mL of ethanol was added to the resultant solution to prevent coagulation of the QDs, and a pale-yellow colored dispersion of B-GQDs was obtained. For the synthesis of N-doped GQDs, an aprotic solvent N,N-Dimethylformamide (DMF) was chosen for increased incorporation of N in the GQD lattice and enhanced optical and electronic properties [29]. Briefly, 210 mg CA and 180 mg urea were dissolved in 5 mL of DMF. The mixture was put into a Teflon-made autoclave chamber, and a solvothermal reaction was accomplished at 200 °C for 8 h. The resultant solution was filtrated, and 2 mL of ethanol was added. Finally, orange-colored dispersion of R-GQDs was obtained. Both samples were stored in the dark for further use.

Investigations on the structural characteristics of the GQDs were carried out using a high-resolution transmission electron microscope (HRTEM) [JEOL, JEM-2100F] equipped with a CCD camera [Gatan Inc. USA]. Characteristic Cu Kα radiation (λ = 1.5418 Å) was used to record a high-resolution X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern [Panalytical MRD] of the samples with a 2.0° grazing angle of incidence. Elemental analysis of the samples was performed with an X-ray photoelectron spectroscope (XPS) equipped with characteristic Al Kα radiation of energy 1486.6 eV [PHI 5000 Versa Probe II, ULVAC PHI, Inc., Japan]. A UV–visible–near-infrared (UV–vis–NIR) spectrophotometer [AvaSpec-3648] was used for optical absorption analysis. Room temperature PL spectra were recorded with excitation from a He–Cd laser of 325 nm wavelength equipped with a monochromator [TRIAX-320] and a PMT detector [Hamamatsu R928]. A broadband light source coupled to a monochromator was used for the excitation-dependent PL measurements. For the PL decay measurements, time-correlated single photon counting (TCSPC) spectroscopy was performed in an Edinburgh LifeSpec-II fluorescence lifetime spectrometer equipped with a PMT detector and a pulsed diode laser of 372 nm wavelength. Raman scattering studies were recorded using a micro-Raman spectrometer (WITec alpha-300R) with a laser of 532 nm wavelength.

A set of commercial Si photodiodes was purchased from Thorlabs (FDS100). Thin films of B-GQDs with varying thicknesses were directly coated on the photodiode, while for optical studies, the same was deposited on quartz substrates. The optoelectronic characteristics were recorded at laboratory ambient using an electrical probe station connected to the Keithley semiconductor parameter analyzer (4200A SCS) along with a fiber-coupled broadband light source and a monochromator. For the UV recognition system, a long pass (LP) filter from Thorlabs (350 nm) and a red-light emitting diode (LED) from a commercial source were used. For the fabrication of the UV sensor, a thin film (∼90 nm) of R-GQDs was initially deposited on a clean glass substrate, followed by the deposition of a relatively thicker layer (∼220 nm) of B-GQDs. At each intermediate step, the film was naturally dried at laboratory ambient. A digital camera was used to capture the emission characteristics of the fabricated devices.

Colloidal GQDs have been synthesized through solvothermal routes, as discussed in the experimental section. Proper choice of precursor salts and solvent to produce GQDs can yield absorption and emission spreading across UV–vis bands. Here, DMF and water have been used to synthesize GQDs with (R-GQDs) or without N-doping (B-GQDs), respectively, and the colloidal solutions appear orange and pale yellow in ambient light. TEM micrographs presented in Fig. 1 a and b shows homogeneous, monodispersed distribution of GQDs over a large area. From the histograms presented in the inset of Fig. 1a and b, the lateral size for B- and R-GQDs is estimated to be 2–6 and 4–8 nm with an average value of ∼4.0 and ∼6.0 nm, respectively. During the reaction, the amide group of urea attaches to the sp3 carbon at a higher rate compared to the core sp2 one in the GQDs [29]. As a result, the concentration of graphitic-N would be lower together with an increased concentration of pyrrolic-N in the N-doped GQDs. From the ordered atomic planes visible in the high-resolution TEM micrograph presented in Figure S1a, Supporting Information file (SI), the interplanar spacing is estimated to be ∼0.24 nm corresponding to (1120) planes of graphitic carbon in graphene [36,37]. Selected area electron diffraction pattern (Figure S1b, SI) of the GQDs reveals impurity-free crystalline GQDs of hexagonal symmetry comparable to that of bulk graphite [36]. These studies confirm the retention of the original crystal symmetry of 2D graphene in the QDs form.

The crystallinity of the as-prepared GQDs has also been examined with an XRD pattern presented in Figure S2, SI, revealing a broad peak around ∼24° associated with (002) planes of few-layer graphene nanostructures [38,39]. The diffraction patterns do not exhibit any feature around ∼10°, excluding the possibility of the formation of graphene oxide. XPS analysis has been carried out to investigate the compositional changes in the samples with N-doping. Elemental survey spectra of the samples shown in Fig. 1c exhibit prominent characteristic peaks for C 1s and O 1s electrons in GQDs at ∼285.0 and ∼531.5 eV, respectively. An additional peak for N 1s electronic states is observed only in the N-doped sample at ∼400 eV [38,39]. The absence of unwanted impurities is evident, suggesting the benefit of the synthesis processes. For detailed analysis, core-level XPS spectra have been recorded in high-resolution mode. The deconvoluted spectrum for C 1s electrons of B-GQDs in Fig. 1d shows three components centered at ∼284.6, ∼286.0, and ∼288.2 eV associated with C–C/C=C bonds, C–OH/C–O–C groups, and COOH (carboxyl groups), respectively [38]. In a similar manner, from Fig. 1e, four peaks are observed for R-GQDs at ∼284.6, ∼285.4, ∼286.4, and ∼288.2 eV. A pronounced contribution at ∼285.4 eV associated with C–N bonds is detected in the N-doped GQDs only [29,38,40]. The association of N atoms into the carbon sp2 matrix in graphene nanostructures can have different C–N bonding configurations. In Fig. 1f, the high-resolution spectra for N 1s electrons have been deconvoluted into two peaks at 400.0 and 401.1 eV, respectively, associated with pyrrolic-N and graphitic-N (see Figure S3a, SI) in N-doped GQDs [29] and the elemental concentration of N is estimated to be ∼12%. This study confirms that N-atoms are indeed incorporated in the C–C bonds of graphene. The ratio of pyrrolic-N to graphitic-N content is estimated to be ∼2.3. A significantly higher contribution from pyrrolic-N compared to graphitic-N is noted and is expected from such an intermediate level of doping. By taking a closer look and comparing highly resolved spectra for N 1s electronic states of doped and undoped GQDs (Figure S3b, SI), the absence of N is confirmed in the undoped sample.

Optical absorption and emission characteristics of GQDs or carbon nanodots are mostly limited to the blue-green wavelength region. Longer wavelength emission characteristics can be achieved through functionalization or doping, but in the majority of cases, the PLQY is reported to be low (<10%) [41]. In this work, a thorough and systematic study of the optical properties of colloidal GQDs has been performed. Fig. 2 a shows that with an intermediate level of N-doping, the absorption and emission features of GQDs can be altered drastically. Visible-blind B-GQDs exhibit peak absorption features in deeper UV regions related to π–π* transition of aromatic sp2 domains [39]. With the incorporation of N, a broader absorption feature covering UV–vis bands is observed in R-GQDs. Interestingly, the doped and undoped samples exhibit relatively broad orange-red and blue emission characteristics (inset of Fig. 2a), respectively, upon excitation with a UV laser (325 nm wavelength), and hence, the samples are named as B- and R-GQDs. Besides having huge Stokes shift in both cases, the PL peak position of the N-doped GQDs is red-shifted to ∼570 nm as compared to the blue emission (∼444 nm) of undoped GQD. The blue emission of undoped B-GQDs originates due to excitonic transitions in the graphitic sp2 carbon cluster, as reported earlier [39]. In the doped case (R-GQDs), the incorporation of nitrous carbon groups dominates the emission characteristics [41,42]. Therefore, a significant change in emission energy of GQDs is realized by N-doping of ∼12%. Quantitatively, the relative (rhodamine B – 80% in ethanol) PLQY of B- and R-GQDs is estimated to be 57 ± 2 and 62 ± 2%, respectively, as shown in Fig. 2b. Various structural and surface defect states can introduce nonradiative pathways leading to reduced emission efficiency [43]. The incorporation of N in the carbon framework, however, increases the rate of radiative recombination, resulting in an enhanced PLQY in R-GQDs considerably. The observation is further validated by PL decay measurements. The charge carrier decay dynamics of the GQDs at their respective peak positions have been recorded by TCSPC, and the fitted results are shown in Fig. 2c. The profile of B-GQDs has been fitted with a bi-exponential decay function, where one fast (
τ
1–1 ns) and another slow decay (
τ
2–5 ns) components with an average time constant of 3 ns could be observed. On the other hand, the decay profile of R-GQDs could be well fitted with single exponential decay model with a time constant ∼1 ns. Interestingly, the faster component in both the cases has similar decay rate suggesting the excitonic nature of the emission from intrinsic states. A relatively higher rate of radiative recombination in R-GQDs is established here in corroboration with the higher PLQY. Considering the self-absorption as a major concern, from the visualization of comparative optical absorption spectra of the doped and undoped GQDs in Fig. 2a, the usage of the orange-red emitting R-GQDs would be imperfect for such visible-blind applications, even though it exhibits higher PLQY (62%) and larger Stokes shift (∼230 nm) compared to B-GQDs. Downconverting scheme with R-GQDs for better UV response could only be derived with significantly degraded performance of a photodetector in the visible region and therefore is excluded. In ambient conditions, as-prepared B-GQDs show long-term homogeneous phase without noticeable precipitation and are able to maintain bright PL emission even for months (Figure S4, SI). After a month, only a drop by 8–10% in PLQY value (see Fig. 2b) suggests the potential of GQDs as an efficient luminescent material. Raman scattering measurements for the samples have been carried out to verify the effect of N-doping and the results are presented in Fig. 2d. Characteristics Raman G- and D-bands are located at 1573 and 1347 cm−1 for B-GQDs, and 1568 and 1354 cm−1 for R-GQDs, respectively [38,42]. A typical broad feature is observed from the samples. The intensity ratio of D-to G-peaks (ID/IG) is estimated to be 0.9 and 1.2 in for B- and R-GQDs, respectively. A larger value of ID/IG ratio for N-doped GQDs compared to graphene suggests the presence of edge and isolated defects in the former, which have distorted sp2 domains associated to heteroatomic effects.

A downconverting scheme harnessing extraordinary optical properties of the UV absorbing GQDs in the visible region for boosted short wavelength detection by a visible photodetector is demonstrated. For this purpose, a commercial Si photodiode was integrated with the B-GQDs to downshift higher energy UV photons into visible ones. Here, the downconverting layer should ideally be transparent to the sensitive band of the detector under test. A layer of B-GQDs as the downconverting layer has been deposited on top of the photodiode for the characterizations, as illustrated in Fig. 3 a. For the external optical studies, a thin film of the GQDs on a quartz substrate has been prepared, and the results of the thorough investigation are presented in Fig. 3b and c. As can be visualized (from the inset of Fig. 3b), the thin film emits bright visible light under excitation with a laser of 325 nm wavelength. Therefore, with the help of GQDs as downconverting materials, it is possible to enhance the UV-photoresponse of a visible-sensitive Si photodetector. The thin film of B-GQDs exhibits strong UV absorption and visible PL with a large stokes shift (∼135 nm). The PL excitation (PLE) spectra (Fig. 3b) respective to the visible peak emission suggests that the UV radiation in the wavelength range of 300–390 nm can be effectively downshifted to the visible band. The thin film exhibits high transparency to the sensitive band of the Si photodiode (∼80% at 500 nm and more than 95% in the range of 600–1100 nm) (Fig. 3c), ensuring minimal degradation of the normal photoresponse. The optical absorption and emission characteristics of the colloidal GQDs do not change significantly compared to the thin film, as can be visualized from Figure S5, SI. The optoelectronic response of the Si photodiode sensitized with B-GQDs has been studied to validate the potential downconversion scheme. Fig. 3d shows the spectral variation of responsivity of the Si photodiode with or without sensitization with GQDs. The UV response of the bare Si photodiode is poor and is found to increase only after sensitization with GQDs. The value of responsivity of the GQD-sensitized photodetector is enhanced to ∼65 from ∼22 mA/W at 320 nm, and therefore, an approximately three-fold enhancement is recorded. The relative increase of responsivity [(R Si + GQD –R Si )/R Si ] at a given wavelength is estimated, and the comparative plot is presented in Fig. 3e. An increase of UV responsivity of over 200% recorded in this study is comparable to the latest results [23–25,44]. The relative increase of responsivity in the measurement bandwidth is a direct consequence of optical absorption and emission in GQDs.

The efficacy of such downconverting scheme depends on a few factors. Upon UV excitation, the layer reemits in all directions (360°), and only a certain portion of visible light can reach the detector. A part of the visible light could be reflected back and forth at the GQDs-detector interface and scatter away [23,35]. In addition, the self-absorption in the downconverting layer could not be eluded as more surface functional groups are attached during the deposition in ambient (Figure S5, SI), increasing visible absorption substantially, thereby undermining the potential of the arrangement. As a result, the enhanced UV photoresponse of the downconverting arrangement is underestimated, and even a slight reduction in photoresponse (<11%) is noted in the region of 400–450 nm (Fig. 3e) [25]. As the film becomes thicker (F3>F2>F1), stronger UV absorption is accompanied by an apparent increase in visible absorption, and as anticipated, an upsurge in PL intensity is prominent (Figure S6, SI) [42]. A closer aspect of the responsivity curve in the UV region from the Si photodetector sensitized with B-GQDs can be visualized in Fig. 3f. With different UV excitations, the downconverting layer strongly absorbs photons in the UV region and reemits in the visible region. Therefore, the GQDs-sensitized Si photodetector maintains an enhanced UV photoresponse. The peak enhancement is found to be at 320 nm, matching closely with the PLE peak (328 nm) of the B-GQDs. After a certain thickness, however, the responsivity value in the UV region starts decreasing owing to the scattering loss of photons [35]. For optimum absorption and reemission, a 140 nm thick film (F2) of B-GQDs has been used in this study (Fig. 3d). The photostability test of the B-GQD thin film has been carried out in a laboratory ambient under continuous exposure to UV light of 365 nm wavelength. The thin film shows a decrease in PL intensity estimated at 87% of its initial value after 55 h of continuous exposure to UV light (Figure S7, SI). This test supports that B-GQDs are suitable for long-lasting downconverting applications.

It is noteworthy to mention that the enhanced value of UV photoresponse is the sum of two contributions. One is the intrinsic photoresponse of the Si photodiode at the particular UV excitation (say 320 nm), and another is the photoresponse at the downshifted emission band at a longer wavelength (centered at 444 nm). The GQD layer cannot effectively absorb the whole UV light (Fig. 3c), leaving some scattered or unabsorbed UV photons that can reach the Si photodiode without going through the downconversion process [23]. Therefore, as a proof of concept, the working of a UV light recognition system [45] is demonstrated, as proposed in Fig. 3g. An LED is connected in series to the unmodified Si photodiode but in forward bias, and it can only glow in the low resistive state of the circuit. The photodiode is mounted parallel to an LP filter and with or without the thin film/dispersion of GQDs. The LP filter will block any UV photons (below 350 nm wavelength) that might pass through the GQDs from reaching the detector. The same has been verified using the arrangement presented in Figure S8a, SI, without the GQD thin film/dispersion. The temporal modulation of current in the circuit is shown in Figure S8b, SI. It can be seen that the intrinsic response of the detector (Si PD) to UV light has been completely cut off by employing the LP filter while it is still responsive to visible excitation. As the detector of the UV recognition system (Fig. 3g) is illuminated with white light and visible light of 514 wavelength, photocurrent passes through the circuit regardless of the presence of the downconverting material, and the LED will be at the ON state, as can be visualized from Fig. 3h. However, when the detector is illuminated with UV light of 325 nm wavelength, the LP filter blocks it, and the LED will be in the OFF state owing to the highly resistive circuit. By inserting the thin films of GQDs (or dispersion in quartz cuvette) before the LP filter, the UV radiation is downshifted to the visible band, and therefore, that light can reach the detector switching the LED to a low conductive ON state. This simple arrangement once again successfully validates the uses of GQDs (doped or undoped) in detecting UV light through the downconversion process. It is needless to mention that the efficacy of such an arrangement is limited, as the light is reemitted in every possible direction, undermining its full potential. A closed optical system like an integrating sphere may help realize better performance [23].

Determination of UV wavelength on a sub-band (UV A–C) basis is crucial for a number of reasons. Utilization of the unreacted UV light in the downconverting layer would play a vital role in the effective downconversion of such UV photons. By applying spectrally tunable GQDs, a working photosensor exhibiting a distinct visible response specific to UV wavelengths is now demonstrated that does not necessarily require coupling with a photodetector and related accessories. Fig. 4 a represents a schematic of the working principle of the UV band sensor on a glass substrate in three possible regimes of operation. The sensor is essentially a downconverting layer composed of a stacked structure of GQDs in tandem fashion, where individual layers could be excited selectively or simultaneously depending on the energy of UV excitation [35]. PL and PLE spectra of the GQDs are compared in Figure S9, SI, where two distinct emission features could be noticed in different regions of the visible band, useful for designing such tandem devices with strong UV absorption. An optimized combination of a relatively thicker (∼220 nm) top layer of B-GQDs and a thin (∼90 nm) bottom layer of R-GQDs is found to be beneficial for better shorter-wavelength UV absorption while maintaining transparency to visible light [35]. In the UV range under test (300–380 nm), three possible mechanisms of light-matter interaction are characterized according to the absorption and subsequent visible emission of the sensor, as shown in Fig. 4b. When UV light of a wavelength in the region of 300–320 nm (UV–B) is used, it selectively excites the top layer of the device (B-GQD), and the resultant blue emission is easily noticeable. A relatively thicker layer of B-GQDs is beneficial to limit shorter-wavelength UV excitation from reaching the bottom layer to a great extent. Alternatively, as the excitation wavelength is in the range of 360–380 nm, it strongly excites the bottom layer of the device (R-GQD), resulting in orange-red emission. In this instance, the top layer of B-GQDs is minimally excited (more than 40% transparency) and contributes weak emission. For excitation in the wavelength range of 330–350 nm, however, both layers are moderately excited resulting in a mixed-colored visible emission. In this region, a significant part of the UV light can pass through the top B-GQD layer without going through any downconversion process or interaction as such, and therefore can reach to the bottom layer (R-GQD) of the device. As the wavelength of UV excitation increases, number of such photons increases as well and the resultant mixed signal is dominated by R-GQDs. Consequently, the emission peak is moving towards longer wavelength side of visible band (orange-red color) with excitation beyond 330 nm wavelength (UV-A). Therefore, depending on the UV excitation (specific to UV-A and -B), different layers could be excited selectively, and distinct colors of emission could be detected by the bare eyes, as shown by the adjacent digital photographs with the variation in PL peak positions in Fig. 4c. Such a device does not require any power source and can successfully determine UV lights on a sub-band basis. Moreover, such devices can also be coupled to any visible photodetector with a suitable arrangement with an integrating sphere [23]. The tunable emission features of the downconverting layer demonstrated here could be advantageous to integrate photodetectors with maximum sensitivity in that particular band to detect UV light, thereby expanding their spectral coverage. Possible integration of the solution-processed GQDs with tunable emission would thus be much more useful for the selective detection of UV light, thereby opening up many opportunities.

Leveraging spectrally tunable optical characteristics of solution-processed GQDs, UV photodetection through the downconversion process has been demonstrated. UV absorbing GQDs with high PLQY (57–62%) alongside a large Stokes shift have been achieved using a simple solvothermal synthesis method, where the optical properties have been tuned by nitrogen functionalization. Integration of such GQDs as a downconverter to downshift UV excitation into visible light for improved and indirect UV detection through a visible Si photodiode is described. An estimated increase of nearly 200% in visible photoresponse from such a downconverting scheme with UV-absorbing B-GQDs is recorded. A UV detection arrangement completely based on the downconversion process is proposed, which further validates the wide applicability of the colloidal GQDs as potential downconverters. The stacked structure of doped and undoped GQDs in a tandem fashion has resulted in distinct visible emissions enabling naked-eye detection of UV lights on a sub-band basis, which does not necessarily require coupling with a photodetector. Tunable optical properties of the solution-processed GQDs are thus useful for selective detection of UV light and extended broadband photoresponse of visible photodetectors.

Subhajit Jana: Conceptualization, Methodology, Investigation, Data curation, Formal analysis, Writing –Original draft; Tamal Dey: Methodology, Investigation, Writing – review & editing; Shivakiran Bhaktha B. N.: Supervision, Validation, Writing – review & editing; Samit K. Ray: Supervision, Validation, Resources, Funding acquisition, Project administration, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare no conflict of interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was partially supported by the Meity-DST-funded NNETRA project at IIT Kharagpur.
